what_it_does: An acceptance test script that exercises runtime.corpus write_record, read_record, list_corpus, and find_corpus against a real FsStore to prove mandatory lineage gating, round-trip persistence, distinct corpus.record event indexing, resume-safe idempotency, and avoidance of consequential event kinds.
observations: Uses a temporary FsStore and restricts itself to existing public store methods.
Defines local check and raises helpers and tracks a global PASS counter.
Fail-loud lineage validation covers None lineage, missing axis, empty axis, and non-dict lineage.
Also validates required output, source_address, and kind fields.
Verifies deterministic addressing via corpus_address with project and projection.
Tests persistence by reading the same record through a fresh FsStore instance.
Asserts index events use CORPUS_EVENT_KIND and that no op.run events are emitted.
Tests find_corpus filters by project, projection, and source_address.
Resume safety is checked via identical CAS, identical address, and deduplicated list_corpus results.
Asserts no forbidden consequential event kinds such as resolve, approve, or dispatch.
Cleans up the temporary store directory in a finally block.
standouts: The headline 'lineage gate' fail-loud validation is the central claim.
Resume-safety is proven through deterministic addressing plus read-time deduplication.
Indexing is explicitly a telemetry corpus.record event, not an op.run or consequential verb.
conventions: Follows the pattern of an acceptance test that is itself presented as a corpus record with lineage metadata.
Uses module-level imperative code with helper functions rather than a unittest/pytest class.
Mutates sys.path and os.chdir at import time to locate the package.
Uses a mutable global counter for pass tracking.
concerns: Global PASS counter and helper side effects make the script less composable.
sys.path manipulation and os.chdir at import time can affect other tests if imported.
Relies on external constants (CORPUS_EVENT_KIND, LINEAGE_FIELDS) without showing their definitions.
Multiple independent concerns are tested in one long sequential script.
notes: An end-to-end acceptance test that treats the corpus-record lineage gate and resume-safe index behavior as the unit under test.
questions: Where are CORPUS_EVENT_KIND, LINEAGE_FIELDS, and corpus_address defined and what are their exact values?
What is the precise semantics of session/round/project for cross-session corroboration?
How does list_corpus implement latest-wins deduplication over index events?
Is this file meant to run standalone, under pytest, or both?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose in the docstring matches the script: it proves lineage gating, round-trip persistence, distinct event indexing, resume-safety, and the floor using only public FsStore methods.
apparent_themes: Deterministic addressing for idempotency and resume safety.
Reuse of existing store primitives instead of adding new storage.
intention_signals: It is trying to establish the corpus record as a first-class, auditable artifact with mandatory lineage, distinct from operational run events, and safe to resume without double-counting.
novelty: The file frames the acceptance test itself as a corpus record with lineage (Cognition Engine D1 · the sequencing gate), blending test metadata with the artifact under test.
contribution: A concrete behavioral contract for corpus records: lineage is mandatory and fail-loud, records are deterministically addressed and resume-safe, and indexing uses a dedicated telemetry event kind rather than operational events.
summary_for_embedding: Acceptance test for runtime.corpus that validates mandatory lineage gating, deterministic record addressing, distinct corpus.record event indexing, and resume-safe deduplication over a real FsStore.
intention_for_embedding: To prove the corpus-record substrate enforces lineage, persists correctly, indexes via telemetry events, and remains idempotent across resume.

symbols.does: Test helper that asserts a condition, increments a global pass counter, and prints an ok/fail message.
symbols.description: A small assertion wrapper used throughout the script to record each acceptance check; it maintains a global PASS counter and fails immediately with a labeled message when the condition is false.

symbols.does: Test helper that expects a callable to raise a given exception and records the result via check.
symbols.description: Wraps a no-argument callable, catches the expected exception type (default CorpusError), and reports success through check; if no exception is raised, it reports failure.
